 {"id":"bd-4y8f","title":"Add btrfs kernel_reference golden tests matching ext4 coverage pattern","description":"## What
Add structured golden-fixture conformance tests for btrfs metadata parsing, matching the
ext4 kernel_reference.rs pattern of: create real image → parse → compare to golden JSON.

## Prior Art (what already exists)
- ext4 has crates/ffs-harness/tests/kernel_reference.rs with multiple test variants:
  creates real mkfs.ext4 images, parses them, validates against golden JSON fixtures
- btrfs E2E tests exist in fuse_e2e.rs (lines 1889-1910) that create mkfs.btrfs images
  and mount them via FUSE
- btrfs conformance fixtures in conformance/fixtures/ are synthetic JSON (not from real images)
- ffs-core unit tests build synthetic btrfs images in-memory (build_btrfs_fsops_image)

## The Specific Gap
btrfs has no equivalent of kernel_reference.rs that:
1. Creates a real btrfs filesystem with known content
2. Parses the superblock, tree structures, and metadata with FrankenFS
3. Compares the parsed output against committed golden JSON fixtures
4. Catches regressions when parsing code changes

## Implementation Plan

### Step 1: Create crates/ffs-harness/tests/btrfs_kernel_reference.rs

fn btrfs_golden_basic_superblock()
  - mkfs.btrfs -f --nodesize 16384 on 256MB sparse file
  - Parse superblock with BtrfsSuperblock::parse_superblock_region()
  - Compare: magic, sectorsize, nodesize, total_bytes, num_devices, csum_type
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_basic_superblock.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_files()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount → create 3 test files with known content → umount
  - Parse: walk root tree → fs tree → verify inode items, dir items, extent data
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_with_files.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_compression()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount -o compress=zlib → create files → umount
  - Parse: verify EXTENT_DATA items have compression type set
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_compressed.json

fn btrfs_golden_with_subvolume()
  - mkfs.btrfs → mount → btrfs subvolume create → add files → umount
  - Parse: verify ROOT_ITEM for subvolume, walk subvol tree
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_subvolume.json

fn btrfs_golden_chunk_and_device_trees()
  - mkfs.btrfs → parse chunk tree entries → verify stripe mapping
  - Golden: conformance/fixtures/btrfs_chunk_tree.json

### Step 2: Create golden fixture files
- Run tests once with REGEN_GOLDENS=1 to generate initial fixtures
- Commit fixtures to conformance/fixtures/btrfs_*.json
- Subsequent runs compare against committed fixtures

### Step 3: Add regeneration support
- scripts/regen_btrfs_goldens.sh: runs tests with REGEN_GOLDENS=1
- Similar to existing scripts/verify_golden.sh for ext4

## Considerations
- mkfs.btrfs minimum image: ~128MB for single-device, use sparse files
- btrfs-progs required: mkfs.btrfs, btrfs (for subvolume create, property set)
- Tests must skip gracefully when btrfs-progs not installed
- Some features (multi-device RAID) need multiple loop devices — skip in CI,
  add as a separate optional test
- Golden format: pretty-printed JSON matching the ext4 golden style
- Mount operations need root or fusermount3 — use btrfs-progs direct manipulation
  where possible (debugfs equivalent: btrfs inspect-internal dump-tree)

## Test Plan
- Each golden test: parse real image → compare to fixture → assert exact match
- Regeneration: REGEN_GOLDENS=1 cargo test -p ffs-harness --test btrfs_kernel_reference
- Verify: changing parsing code causes golden mismatch (regression detection works)
- Skip tests gracefully when mkfs.btrfs unavailable

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 5 btrfs golden conformance tests implemented and passing
- [ ] Golden JSON fixtures committed to conformance/fixtures/
- [ ] Tests create real mkfs.btrfs images (not synthetic)
- [ ] Regression detection verified: intentional parse change → test failure
- [ ] Tests skip gracefully when btrfs-progs unavailable
- [ ] Regeneration script works","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-06T15:49:25.746184725Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-06T23:05:11.260412024Z","closed_at":"2026-04-06T23:05:11.260211284Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-k9zm","title":"Apply fast commit operations to filesystem metadata during mount","description":"BACKGROUND: ext4 fast commit (FC) is a lightweight journal mechanism that records individual filesystem operations (create, link, unlink, add_range, del_range, inode update) instead of full block snapshots. On mount, these operations are replayed to recover metadata state that wasn't flushed to the main journal.

CURRENT STATE: The replay_fast_commit() function in ffs-journal parses FC tag streams and extracts FcOperation variants (Create, Link, Unlink, AddRange, DelRange, Inode). The mount-time path (maybe_collect_ext4_fast_commit_evidence in ffs-core) reads these operations and stores them as Ext4FastCommitReplayEvidence — purely observational.

FEATURE_PARITY.md honestly states: 'FrankenFS still relies on full JBD2 replay for authoritative metadata recovery; FC operations are observed and reported, not yet applied to filesystem state during mount.'

WHAT NEEDS TO HAPPEN:
1. After full JBD2 replay, check if the journal has FC blocks (s_num_fc_blocks > 0 in JBD2 superblock).
2. Parse the FC tag stream from the journal's FC tail region.
3. For each committed FC transaction (HEAD...TAIL pair):
   a. Create: allocate inode, add dir entry to parent.
   b. Link: add dir entry to parent (inode already exists).
   c. Unlink: remove dir entry from parent.
   d. AddRange: insert extent mapping for the inode.
   e. DelRange: remove extent mapping range.
   f. Inode: update inode metadata (size, timestamps, etc.).
4. Write the updated metadata blocks to disk.
5. Clear the FC area in the journal superblock.

COMPLEXITY: High. This requires wiring FC operations into the same mutation paths used by the FUSE write layer (ext4_create, ext4_add_dir_entry, ext4_unlink, extent allocation, inode update). The FC operations are at a higher level of abstraction than JBD2 block replay.

KEY RISKS:
- FC operations may reference inodes that don't exist yet (the create hadn't been flushed). Need to handle this gracefully.
- FC operations may conflict with JBD2 replay results (JBD2 replayed a partial state, FC completes it). Need idempotent application.
- Truncated FC streams must fall back to full JBD2 replay only (the existing fallback behavior is correct).

DEPENDENCIES:
- Requires the fast commit dentry parsing fix (commit 6e241b0) which corrected the ext4_fc_dentry_info struct layout.
- Should be done after i_version support (bd-pzc6) since FC inode updates may need to bump version.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:

- A test with a truncated FC stream (no TAIL) falls back to JBD2-only replay.
- FEATURE_PARITY.md updated to remove the 'observed and reported, not yet applied' caveat.
- Evidence logging records which FC operations were applied.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:06:17.638537685Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:49:44.306396946Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:49:44.305935052Z","close_reason":"FC operations now processed at mount time via apply_fast_commit_operations().  {"id":"bd-qpt5","title":"Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation","description":"# Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation

## GOAL
Accelerate CRC32C and BLAKE3 checksum computation using SIMD instructions.

## BACKGROUND (Extreme Software Optimization)
Checksum computation is on the hot path:
- Every block read/write verifies checksum
- ARC cache hits still verify (trust but verify)
- RaptorQ symbols have checksums

SIMD can provide 4-16x speedup depending on instruction set.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. CRC32C Acceleration
```rust
pub fn crc32c_simd(data: &[u8], seed: u32) -> u32 {
    #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
    {
        if is_x86_feature_detected!("sse4.2") {
            return crc32c_sse42(data, seed);
        }
    }
    #[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
    {
        if is_aarch64_feature_detected!("crc") {
            return crc32c_arm_crc(data, seed);
        }
    }
    crc32c_fallback(data, seed)
}
```

### 2. BLAKE3 (Already SIMD)
- blake3 crate already uses SIMD
- Ensure proper feature flags enabled
- Benchmark to confirm

### 3. Batch Checksum
```rust
/// Compute checksums for multiple blocks in parallel
pub fn batch_checksum(blocks: &[&[u8]], algo: ChecksumAlgo) -> Vec<u32> {
    // Use rayon for multi-block parallelism
    // Use SIMD within each block
}
```

### 4. Feature Detection at Runtime
- Detect CPU features once at startup
- Store in global (or thread-local)
- Dispatch to appropriate implementation

## TESTS
1. Unit: SIMD matches fallback results
2. Unit: All supported CPUs tested (via CI matrix)
3. Benchmark: Before/after comparison
4. Property: Random data, SIMD == fallback

## LOGGING
- Feature detection (info): detected SIMD capabilities
- Batch checksum (trace): block count, duration

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Correctness: SIMD == fallback for all inputs
2. Performance: >2x speedup on supported CPUs
3. Graceful fallback on unsupported CPUs
4. No unsafe code (use safe SIMD wrappers)

## NOTES
Use existing crates:
- crc32c crate has SIMD support
- blake3 crate is already optimized
Main work is ensuring proper feature flags and benchmarking.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:55:14.096946312Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163955333Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163936237Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["optimization","perf","simd"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:00.533583752Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:29.930946133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":207,"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-xvoe","title":"Investigate and fix ext4 bitmap CRC32C mismatch against real mkfs.ext4 images","description":"PROBLEM: The kernel_reference integration test ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel fails because our computed block bitmap checksum (via verify_block_bitmap_checksum -> block_bitmap_checksum_value -> ext4_bitmap_checksum) does not match the checksum stored by mkfs.ext4 1.47.2 on real images.

EVIDENCE: On a fresh 8MB ext4 image (mkfs.ext4 -L test -b 4096 -q -O ^has_journal), seed=0xba6605a8, our code computes 0xcd45611c but the stored checksum is 0xb8339b40. The ext4_chksum bridge function (!crc32c_append(!raw, data)) is verified correct for known test vectors (b'hello' produces the expected result). The GDT field offsets (bg_block_bitmap_csum_lo at 0x18, bg_block_bitmap_csum_hi at 0x38) match the kernel struct. The csum_seed derivation (from s_checksum_seed when CSUM_SEED is set) is verified correct.

POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSES:
(a) The crc32c crate 0.6.8 may have a hardware-acceleration edge case on this CPU (the test passes for small inputs but may diverge on 4096-byte inputs).
(b) There may be a subtle difference in how the kernel chains CRC32C for bitmap checksums vs how we do it (e.g., the kernel might zero some bits in the bitmap before checksumming, or exclude the last N bytes).
(c) The group descriptor checksum field offset may differ between mkfs.ext4 versions.
(d) There's a SECOND bitmap checksum function (compute_bitmap_checksum at line 1743) that uses csum_seed^group (XOR) instead of chaining — this function is wrong but verify_block_bitmap_checksum doesn't use it. However, it may have been copy-pasted from or to somewhere unexpected.

INVESTIGATION PLAN:
1. Add a test that creates a fresh ext4 image with mkfs.ext4, reads the raw bitmap bytes, and computes our CRC step-by-step, printing intermediate values.
2. Cross-validate by computing the same CRC using a second Rust CRC32C implementation (e.g., crc32c-hw or manual software CRC).
3. If the crate is correct, hexdump the raw GDT bytes and verify our parser reads the stored checksum from the right offset.
4. Check if mkfs.ext4 zeroes any bitmap bits before checksumming (e.g., unused tail bits beyond blocks_per_group).
5. Check the kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() to see if it modifies the bitmap before checksumming.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel test passes on a fresh mkfs.ext4 image.
- The root cause is documented in a code comment.
- If the crc32c crate has a bug, switch to an alternative or add a workaround.
- The wrong compute_bitmap_checksum function (XOR-based) is either removed or clearly marked as deprecated.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:10.024539600Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535625271Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535051315Z","close_reason":"Root cause: (1) group_number was chained into bitmap CRC but kernel doesn't do this, (2) checksum length was full block instead of clusters/inodes_per_group/8.  {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization. Property test: rank(select(k)) == k for all valid k.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralThrush","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:30:45.958783809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736127822Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736029688Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datastructure","freespace","succinct"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:05:32.714682331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":202,"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).
